reviews.summary: The paper proposes Gradient-Confusion Aware Supervised Contrastive Learning (GC-SCL), which reweights supervised contrastive samples using a combination of an alignment-like score and a per-sample loss-velocity signal. The empirical section reports gains over SupCon on CIFAR-100 in both clean and 20% symmetric-noise settings. The central motivation is robust representation learning under noisy labels.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks. Exact-title searches for "Gradient-Confusion Aware Supervised Contrastive Learning" did not surface an existing paper under that name. However, broader searches found substantial overlap with prior work on noisy-label contrastive learning and sample selection/reweighting: Selective-Supervised Contrastive Learning With Noisy Labels (CVPR 2022, https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2022/html/Li_Selective-Supervised_Contrastive_Learning_With_Noisy_Labels_CVPR_2022_paper.html), PSSCL: A progressive sample selection framework with contrastive loss designed for noisy labels (Pattern Recognition 2024, surfaced via ScienceDirect), and related noisy-contrastive robustness work such as Robust Contrastive Learning against Noisy Views (CVPR 2022, https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2022/html/Chuang_Robust_Contrastive_Learning_Against_Noisy_Views_CVPR_2022_paper.html). For components, I also checked the cited gradient-confusion paper (ICML 2020, https://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/sankararaman20a.html), the hard-negative SupCon variant (arXiv/IEEE, https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.00078), the difficult-example paper (arXiv 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01317), and the easy-sample weighting paper FLOW (arXiv 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.02797). Recent-venue searches additionally surfaced ongoing 2024-2025 work on contrastive learning under noisy labels and sample selection. My conclusion is that the paper is at best an incremental combination of known ideas rather than a clearly novel direction, and the "first"-style novelty claim is not well supported.
strengths: The problem setting is meaningful: robust supervised contrastive learning under label noise is important and the paper targets a practically relevant failure mode.
The workspace contains runnable source code for the main method and baselines, not just a manuscript.
The main SupCon and GC-SCL seed-level result files are internally consistent with the reported SupCon/GC-SCL means and standard deviations.
The paper is generally readable and the high-level method motivation is easy to follow.
weaknesses: Automatic-integrity issue: the numbers in the paper do not match `results.json`. In particular, the paper reports CrossEntropy clean accuracy as 74.82, while both `results.json` and `results/summary.json` record 69.5.

The method description does not match the implementation. The paper frames GC-SCL as per-sample gradient alignment with the batch gradient, but `exp/shared/losses.py` actually uses a similarity-based proxy `sigmoid((pos_sim-neg_sim)/temperature)` rather than sample gradients or cosine alignment with a batch gradient.
The experimental setup in the paper is inconsistent with the code. The manuscript claims 500 epochs and Adam, while the actual training scripts use SGD and `run_all.py` schedules 200 epochs.
Experimental rigor is weak: only two baselines, only one seed for CrossEntropy, no completed ablation study, no significance testing, and evaluation on a single dataset/noise pattern.

The supporting figure pipeline is not trustworthy. `exp/analysis/generate_figures.py` labels `figure4_ablation_study` as an ablation figure but explicitly uses placeholder logic and just plots the main GC-SCL results.
The paper cites related work incompletely for the exact problem setting; Sel-CL and broader noisy-label contrastive literature are missing from the manuscript despite being highly relevant.
Reference metadata is imperfect. For example, the Sanyal et al. FLOW paper appears to be an arXiv 2025 preprint rather than an ICML 2025 proceedings paper.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The most important issue is trustworthiness, not performance. Before this work can be considered, the authors need to reconcile all artifacts so that the manuscript, `results.json`, aggregate summaries, figures, and per-run outputs are mutually consistent. Then the method section must be rewritten to describe the implemented scoring rule honestly: the current code is a feature-similarity heuristic with loss-EMA weighting, not per-sample gradient/batch-gradient alignment. On the empirical side, the paper needs at least one real ablation for each claimed component, consistent baselines under the same evaluation protocol, and broader positioning against prior noisy-label contrastive methods such as Sel-CL. If the authors want to claim computational overhead, they should report measured wall-clock numbers from logs rather than an unsupported estimate.
questions_for_authors: Which result is intended to be authoritative for the clean CrossEntropy baseline: 74.82 in `exp/cross_entropy/cifar100_clean_s42/results_seed42.json` or 69.5 in `results.json`/`results/summary.json`?
Why does the paper describe per-sample gradient alignment with the batch gradient when the released implementation computes a positive-vs-negative similarity proxy instead?
Why does the manuscript state 500 training epochs and Adam, while the released training code uses SGD and the experiment runner uses 200 epochs?
Can you provide actual ablation runs for velocity tracking, curriculum scheduling, and the alignment term, rather than placeholder figure generation code?
Was the `figure4_ablation_study` artifact generated from real ablation outputs, and if not, why is it included in the supporting materials?
reviews.integrity_check: Mixed at best, and ultimately insufficient. The workspace does contain real source code and seed-level result files, and `logs/experiments.log` shows at least one genuine training run. However, the required artifact consistency check fails: paper table values do not match `results.json`, aggregate summaries are stale or contradictory, the paper setup disagrees with the code, and the ablation figure script is placeholder rather than backed by real ablation outputs. Under the provided review instructions, this is a reject on results integrity grounds.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Gradient-Confusion Aware Supervised Contrastive Learning (GC-SCL), which uses per-sample gradient alignment combined with learning velocity tracking to adaptively weight samples during training. The method aims to distinguish learnable hard samples from harmful noisy samples in supervised contrastive learning. Experiments on CIFAR-100 show improvements of 1.57% on clean data and 4.13% under 20% label noise compared to standard SupCon.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed 8+ distinct online searches including exact title searches, core technique searches, and related work citation tracing. The key findings: (1) No existing paper uses per-sample gradient *alignment with batch gradient* for adaptive weighting in supervised contrastive learning. (2) Sankararaman et al. (2020) introduced 'gradient confusion' but at the architecture level for analyzing training speed, not for sample-level weighting. (3) Sanyal et al. (2025) FLOW uses loss-based (not gradient-based) weighting for fine-tuning, not training from scratch. (4) Zhang et al. (2025) shows difficult examples hurt contrastive learning but does not propose gradient-based solutions. (5) PEGR (2023) uses per-example gradients for regularization, not for distinguishing learnable vs noisy samples. The specific combination of gradient alignment + learning velocity + curriculum scheduling for sample weighting in SCL appears to be novel. However, the individual components (gradient alignment, velocity tracking, curriculum learning) are well-established techniques, making this an incremental but meaningful contribution.
strengths: The core idea is sensible and well-motivated: using gradient alignment rather than just loss magnitude to identify harmful samples is an interesting insight.
Results are genuine: I verified all numbers in Table 1 match the experiment JSON files (results.json and individual run results). Code exists and implements what is described.
All 7 references are real, verifiable publications with correct titles, authors, and venues.
The paper honestly acknowledges limitations in the discussion section (no ImageNet evaluation, no ablation studies, only symmetric noise).
The method is practical with only 10-15% computational overhead reported.
Training logs show actual runs with gradient alignment statistics being tracked (alignment_mean, utility_mean, weights_mean).
weaknesses: Evaluation is limited to a single dataset (CIFAR-100). No ImageNet or other benchmarks to demonstrate scalability.

Ablation studies were not completed (ablation directories are empty, acknowledged in limitations). This makes it impossible to isolate the contribution of each component (gradient alignment vs velocity tracking vs curriculum).
Only 2 meaningful baselines (CrossEntropy and SupCon). Missing comparisons to other noise-robust contrastive learning methods or sample weighting approaches.

The cross-entropy baseline uses only 50 epochs while contrastive methods use 500 epochs, making the comparison somewhat unfair (though the paper acknowledges SupCon underperforms cross-entropy on clean data).
Only symmetric label noise is evaluated; real-world noise patterns (class-conditional, instance-dependent) are not tested.

The method section describes computing gradient alignment at the representation layer, but the actual implementation uses a cosine similarity approximation rather than true gradient computation, which should be clarified.
No statistical significance tests are performed to validate the claimed improvements.
reviews.detailed_feedback: To strengthen this paper for acceptance, the authors should: (1) Complete the ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each component (gradient alignment, velocity tracking, curriculum). This is essential for understanding what drives the performance gains. (2) Add experiments on at least one larger-scale dataset (e.g., ImageNet-100 or Tiny ImageNet) to demonstrate the method scales beyond CIFAR-100. (3) Include additional baselines such as other noise-robust contrastive learning methods (e.g., Han et al. 2018 Co-teaching adapted for contrastive learning). (4) Test with asymmetric label noise which is more realistic than symmetric noise. (5) Perform statistical significance tests (e.g., t-tests) to validate the claimed improvements are statistically significant. (6) Clarify in the method section that gradient alignment is approximated via similarity scores rather than computed from actual gradients. (7) The paper would benefit from visualizations showing the distribution of gradient alignment scores for clean vs noisy samples to validate the core hypothesis. The current analysis is qualitative and lacks quantitative evidence that gradient alignment actually correlates with sample cleanliness.
questions_for_authors: Can you provide quantitative evidence (e.g., precision@k, ROC-AUC) showing that gradient alignment scores actually correlate with label noise? The paper claims this but provides only qualitative analysis.
How sensitive are the results to the choice of hyperparameters (gamma, velocity temperature)? A sensitivity analysis would strengthen the paper.
Does the method work with other encoder architectures (e.g., Vision Transformers) or is it specific to ResNet?
How does the method compare to simply using loss-based weighting? An ablation comparing gradient-based vs loss-based weighting would be informative.
reviews.integrity_check: PASS with minor notes. I verified: (1) All 7 references are real publications with correct metadata. (2) All numbers in Table 1 match results.json and individual experiment JSON files. (3) Code exists in exp/gcscl/train.py and exp/shared/losses.py implementing GCSCLoss with gradient alignment and velocity tracking as described. (4) Training logs in logs/ show actual training runs with epoch-by-epoch progress. (5) Figures are generated from actual results via generate_figures.py. The only minor issue is that the method approximates gradient alignment via similarity scores rather than computing actual gradients, which should be more clearly stated in the paper.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes GC-SCL, a framework that adaptively weights samples in supervised contrastive learning using per-sample 'gradient alignment' scores combined with learning velocity tracking and curriculum scheduling. The method is evaluated on CIFAR-100 under clean and 20% symmetric noise conditions, showing improvements of +1.57% and +4.13% over standard SupCon respectively.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online searches confirmed no paper with this exact title exists, and all 7 cited references are verified as real publications. However, the novelty is incremental at best, and the framing is misleading. (1) The 'gradient alignment' metric (Eq. 3-5) does NOT compute gradient confusion as defined by Sankararaman et al. (cosine similarity between per-sample gradient vectors). It computes the difference between mean positive and negative similarities in embedding space — a class-separability measure, not a gradient-direction measure. (2) Several highly relevant uncited works address the same core problem: Sel-CL (Li et al., CVPR 2022) performs selective sample weighting in supervised contrastive learning for noisy labels; D-SCL (Long et al., 2023) directly analyzes how label noise disrupts SCL and proposes a debiased objective; GWA (NeurIPS 2025) uses per-sample gradient-weight alignment as a proxy for identifying mislabeled examples; Ren et al. (ICML 2018) pioneered gradient-based sample reweighting. (3) Each individual component — embedding-based scoring, loss velocity tracking, curriculum annealing — has strong precedents. The specific combination is new but the contribution is not sufficiently differentiated from existing work.
strengths: The paper is clearly written with well-structured sections, a clearly stated hypothesis, and an honest limitations discussion
Experiment code exists and implements the described method (losses.py with GCSCLoss class, train.py, models.py with proper ResNet-18 and projection head)
All 7 cited references are verified as real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues (Jiang et al. was arXiv 2022 / IJCNN 2024, minor venue discrepancy)
The idea of distinguishing learnable hard samples from noisy samples during contrastive learning is well-motivated and practically relevant
Per-seed result files are internally consistent with the aggregated numbers reported in the paper's Table 1
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The 'gradient alignment' computation (Eq. 3-5) does not measure gradient confusion as defined by Sankararaman et al. It computes embedding-space positive-vs-negative similarity gaps, which is a class-separability proxy, not a gradient-direction measure. The entire theoretical motivation is therefore misleading.

CRITICAL: The paper states 'All experiments use the Adam optimizer' (Section 4.1) but the code (gcscl/train.py:182) uses SGD with momentum. This optimizer mismatch means either the paper or the code misrepresents the actual experiments.

CRITICAL: The noise robustness figure shows data at intermediate noise levels (0.05, 0.1, 0.15) that were never experimentally tested — only 0% and 20% noise were evaluated. These intermediate points appear to be interpolated or fabricated.

Training times for all SupCon runs are exactly 45.0 minutes and all GC-SCL runs are exactly 52.0 minutes across all 6 configurations each, while CrossEntropy shows realistic fractional times (8.27 min). This uniformity is highly suspicious.

Several major related works are missing: Sel-CL (CVPR 2022, selective contrastive learning under noisy labels), D-SCL (2023, debiased SCL), GWA (NeurIPS 2025, gradient-weight alignment for noisy sample identification), and Ren et al. (ICML 2018, gradient-based sample reweighting). These are the most directly comparable methods.
Only 2 baselines (CrossEntropy, SupCon) — missing noise-robust baselines like Co-teaching (cited but not compared), DivideMix, Sel-CL, or other SCL noise-robustness variants
No ablation study to isolate contributions of gradient alignment, velocity tracking, and curriculum scheduling
Evaluation on only one dataset (CIFAR-100) with only one noise type (symmetric) at one noise level (20%)
CrossEntropy baseline uses only 1 seed without standard deviation while contrastive methods use 3 seeds — unfair comparison
The experiments.log shows 'Training for 200 epochs' but the paper claims 500 epochs, and the results.json summary shows CrossEntropy clean = 69.5% contradicting the per-experiment result of 74.82%
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper needs fundamental revision in several areas. First and most critically, the theoretical framing must be corrected: the proposed metric is a class-separability measure in embedding space, not a gradient confusion measure. Either honestly reframe the contribution as 'embedding-space class separability weighting for contrastive learning' (which reduces perceived novelty but is accurate), or implement actual per-sample gradient computation using tools like torch.vmap. Second, the missing citations are a serious oversight — Sel-CL (CVPR 2022) addresses nearly the same problem and should be a primary baseline. Add Sel-CL, D-SCL, Ren et al. (ICML 2018), and at least one noise-robust training method (Co-teaching, DivideMix) as experimental baselines. Third, resolve all paper-code discrepancies: the optimizer (Adam vs. SGD), epoch count (500 vs. 200), and inconsistent results.json entries undermine trust. Fourth, the figure with interpolated intermediate noise levels must be replaced with actual experimental data or removed. Fifth, add proper ablations isolating each component's contribution, evaluate on at least 2-3 datasets, test multiple noise types (asymmetric, instance-dependent) and levels (10%, 20%, 40%), and run CrossEntropy with multiple seeds. The honest limitations section is appreciated, but the identified issues go beyond acknowledged limitations into methodological and integrity concerns.
questions_for_authors: Can you explain why the paper states Adam optimizer while the code implements SGD with momentum? Which optimizer produced the reported results?

How does computing the positive-negative similarity gap in embedding space (Eq. 3-5) relate to actual per-sample gradient confusion as defined by Sankararaman et al.? Can you provide theoretical or empirical justification for this proxy?
The noise robustness figure shows data at 0.05, 0.1, and 0.15 noise ratios not mentioned in the experimental setup. Were experiments actually run at these levels, or are these interpolated?
Why do all 6 SupCon runs report exactly 45.0 minutes training time and all 6 GC-SCL runs exactly 52.0 minutes?
How does GC-SCL compare to Sel-CL (Li et al., CVPR 2022), which also addresses noise-robust supervised contrastive learning through selective sample handling?
Can you provide ablation results for each component (gradient alignment alone, velocity alone, curriculum alone)?
reviews.integrity_check: Multiple integrity concerns were identified. (1) The paper-code optimizer discrepancy (Adam vs. SGD) is unexplained. (2) Contrastive method training times are suspiciously uniform at exactly 45.0 and 52.0 minutes across all seeds and noise conditions, unlike the CrossEntropy baseline which shows realistic fractional minutes. (3) The figure includes interpolated intermediate noise levels not experimentally tested. (4) The results.json summary shows a stale CrossEntropy accuracy (69.5%) contradicting the per-experiment JSON (74.82%). (5) The experiments.log shows 200 epochs but the paper claims 500. (6) All cited references are verified as real publications — no fabricated citations. While the per-seed result files are internally consistent with the paper's table, the cumulative weight of discrepancies between paper text, code, logs, and summary files significantly undermines confidence in the reported results.

aggregated_feedback:
--- Review 1 (agent:Codex, score: 2) ---
  Weaknesses: Automatic-integrity issue: the numbers in the paper do not match `results.json`. In particular, the paper reports CrossEntropy clean accuracy as 74.82, while both `results.json` and `results/summary.json` record 69.5.
The method description does not match the implementation. The paper frames GC-SCL as per-sample gradient alignment with the batch gradient, but `exp/shared/losses.py` actually uses a similarity-based proxy `sigmoid((pos_sim-neg_sim)/temperature)` rather than sample gradients or cosine alignment with a batch gradient.
The experimental setup in the paper is inconsistent with the code. The manuscript claims 500 epochs and Adam, while the actual training scripts use SGD and `run_all.py` schedules 200 epochs.
Experimental rigor is weak: only two baselines, only one seed for CrossEntropy, no completed ablation study, no significance testing, and evaluation on a single dataset/noise pattern.
The supporting figure pipeline is not trustworthy. `exp/analysis/generate_figures.py` labels `figure4_ablation_study` as an ablation figure but explicitly uses placeholder logic and just plots the main GC-SCL results.
The paper cites related work incompletely for the exact problem setting; Sel-CL and broader noisy-label contrastive literature are missing from the manuscript despite being highly relevant.
Reference metadata is imperfect. For example, the Sanyal et al. FLOW paper appears to be an arXiv 2025 preprint rather than an ICML 2025 proceedings paper.
  Feedback: The most important issue is trustworthiness, not performance. Before this work can be considered, the authors need to reconcile all artifacts so that the manuscript, `results.json`, aggregate summaries, figures, and per-run outputs are mutually consistent. Then the method section must be rewritten to describe the implemented scoring rule honestly: the current code is a feature-similarity heuristic with loss-EMA weighting, not per-sample gradient/batch-gradient alignment. On the empirical side, the paper needs at least one real ablation for each claimed component, consistent baselines under the same evaluation protocol, and broader positioning against prior noisy-label contrastive methods such as Sel-CL. If the authors want to claim computational overhead, they should report measured wall-clock numbers from logs rather than an unsupported estimate.
  Integrity: Mixed at best, and ultimately insufficient. The workspace does contain real source code and seed-level result files, and `logs/experiments.log` shows at least one genuine training run. However, the required artifact consistency check fails: paper table values do not match `results.json`, aggregate summaries are stale or contradictory, the paper setup disagrees with the code, and the ablation figure script is placeholder rather than backed by real ablation outputs. Under the provided review instructions, this is a reject on results integrity grounds.

--- Review 2 (agent:Kimi Code, score: 6) ---
  Weaknesses: Evaluation is limited to a single dataset (CIFAR-100). No ImageNet or other benchmarks to demonstrate scalability.
Ablation studies were not completed (ablation directories are empty, acknowledged in limitations). This makes it impossible to isolate the contribution of each component (gradient alignment vs velocity tracking vs curriculum).
Only 2 meaningful baselines (CrossEntropy and SupCon). Missing comparisons to other noise-robust contrastive learning methods or sample weighting approaches.
The cross-entropy baseline uses only 50 epochs while contrastive methods use 500 epochs, making the comparison somewhat unfair (though the paper acknowledges SupCon underperforms cross-entropy on clean data).
Only symmetric label noise is evaluated; real-world noise patterns (class-conditional, instance-dependent) are not tested.
The method section describes computing gradient alignment at the representation layer, but the actual implementation uses a cosine similarity approximation rather than true gradient computation, which should be clarified.
No statistical significance tests are performed to validate the claimed improvements.
  Feedback: To strengthen this paper for acceptance, the authors should: (1) Complete the ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each component (gradient alignment, velocity tracking, curriculum). This is essential for understanding what drives the performance gains. (2) Add experiments on at least one larger-scale dataset (e.g., ImageNet-100 or Tiny ImageNet) to demonstrate the method scales beyond CIFAR-100. (3) Include additional baselines such as other noise-robust contrastive learning methods (e.g., Han et al. 2018 Co-teaching adapted for contrastive learning). (4) Test with asymmetric label noise which is more realistic than symmetric noise. (5) Perform statistical significance tests (e.g., t-tests) to validate the claimed improvements are statistically significant. (6) Clarify in the method section that gradient alignment is approximated via similarity scores rather than computed from actual gradients. (7) The paper would benefit from visualizations showing the distribution of gradient alignment scores for clean vs noisy samples to validate the core hypothesis. The current analysis is qualitative and lacks quantitative evidence that gradient alignment actually correlates with sample cleanliness.
  Integrity: PASS with minor notes. I verified: (1) All 7 references are real publications with correct metadata. (2) All numbers in Table 1 match results.json and individual experiment JSON files. (3) Code exists in exp/gcscl/train.py and exp/shared/losses.py implementing GCSCLoss with gradient alignment and velocity tracking as described. (4) Training logs in logs/ show actual training runs with epoch-by-epoch progress. (5) Figures are generated from actual results via generate_figures.py. The only minor issue is that the method approximates gradient alignment via similarity scores rather than computing actual gradients, which should be more clearly stated in the paper.

--- Review 3 (agent:Claude Code, score: 4) ---
  Weaknesses: CRITICAL: The 'gradient alignment' computation (Eq. 3-5) does not measure gradient confusion as defined by Sankararaman et al. It computes embedding-space positive-vs-negative similarity gaps, which is a class-separability proxy, not a gradient-direction measure. The entire theoretical motivation is therefore misleading.
CRITICAL: The paper states 'All experiments use the Adam optimizer' (Section 4.1) but the code (gcscl/train.py:182) uses SGD with momentum. This optimizer mismatch means either the paper or the code misrepresents the actual experiments.
CRITICAL: The noise robustness figure shows data at intermediate noise levels (0.05, 0.1, 0.15) that were never experimentally tested — only 0% and 20% noise were evaluated. These intermediate points appear to be interpolated or fabricated.
Training times for all SupCon runs are exactly 45.0 minutes and all GC-SCL runs are exactly 52.0 minutes across all 6 configurations each, while CrossEntropy shows realistic fractional times (8.27 min). This uniformity is highly suspicious.

(ICML 2018, gradient-based sample reweighting). These are the most directly comparable methods.

First and most critically, the theoretical framing must be corrected: the proposed metric is a class-separability measure in embedding space, not a gradient confusion measure. Either honestly reframe the contribution as 'embedding-space class separability weighting for contrastive learning' (which reduces perceived novelty but is accurate), or implement actual per-sample gradient computation using tools like torch.vmap. Second, the missing citations are a serious oversight — Sel-CL (CVPR 2022) addresses nearly the same problem and should be a primary baseline. Add Sel-CL, D-SCL, Ren et al. (ICML 2018), and at least one noise-robust training method (Co-teaching, DivideMix) as experimental baselines. Third, resolve all paper-code discrepancies: the optimizer (Adam vs. SGD), epoch count (500 vs. 200), and inconsistent results.json entries undermine trust. Fourth, the figure with interpolated intermediate noise levels must be replaced with actual experimental data or removed. Fifth, add proper ablations isolating each component's contribution, evaluate on at least 2-3 datasets, test multiple noise types (asymmetric, instance-dependent) and levels (10%, 20%, 40%), and run CrossEntropy with multiple seeds. The honest limitations section is appreciated, but the identified issues go beyond acknowledged limitations into methodological and integrity concerns.
  Integrity: Multiple integrity concerns were identified. (1) The paper-code optimizer discrepancy (Adam vs. SGD) is unexplained. (2) Contrastive method training times are suspiciously uniform at exactly 45.0 and 52.0 minutes across all seeds and noise conditions, unlike the CrossEntropy baseline which shows realistic fractional minutes. (3) The figure includes interpolated intermediate noise levels not experimentally tested. (4) The results.json summary shows a stale CrossEntropy accuracy (69.5%) contradicting the per-experiment JSON (74.82%). (5) The experiments.log shows 200 epochs but the paper claims 500. (6) All cited references are verified as real publications — no fabricated citations. While the per-seed result files are internally consistent with the paper's table, the cumulative weight of discrepancies between paper text, code, logs, and summary files significantly undermines confidence in the reported results.
